1:
"Paul, and Silvanus, and Timotheus, unto the church of the Thessalonians in God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ:" The brief verse notes the greeting from the three to the church in God and Christ. The grammatical voices note the senders. Barnes and Robertson note that Paul, as in the first epistle, joins "Silvanus (Silas) and Timotheus (Timothy)" with himself — the three who had founded the church together; he writes again to the same beloved congregation, without adding his apostolic title, on a footing of affectionate familiarity.

On "unto the church of the Thessalonians in God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the address. Barnes and Calvin note the distinctive phrase: the church "in God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" — its life and being rooted in God and Christ together; the Father and the Son named as the sphere of its existence, a witness to the Son's place beside the Father.

The grammatical-historical note the continuity: this second letter follows close upon the first, occasioned by new questions about the day of the Lord; the same three write to the same church. The verse displays the greeting from the three to the church in God and Christ.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul joins "Silvanus (Silas) and Timotheus (Timothy)," the three founders, writing again without his apostolic title (Barnes, Robertson); the church "in God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ," its being rooted in God and Christ, the Son beside the Father (Barnes, Calvin); the second letter following close on the first (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the greeting. The verse marks the senders: Paul, Silvanus, Timothy. The verse displays the church: in God and Christ. The verse displays the continuity: a second letter. The verse displays the address: the Thessalonians. The greeting displays the three saluting the church rooted in God. The verse displays the senders: three. The verse displays the church: theirs. The verse displays the God: Father and Son. The greeting from the three to the church in God and Christ displays the opening of Paul's second letter: Paul, Silas, and Timothy salute again the Thessalonian church — whose life is rooted in God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ — taking up afresh the questions their first letter had raised. The verse displays the apostolic greeting, completed by grace and peace (v.2).
2:
"Grace unto you, and peace, from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ." The brief verse notes the apostolic blessing of grace and peace. The grammatical voices note the salutation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's customary greeting: "grace (free favour) unto you, and peace (its fruit)" — the twofold blessing that opens his letters, grace the source and peace the effect.

On "from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the joint source. Barnes and Gill note that grace and peace come "from God our Father (the fountain of all blessing) and the Lord Jesus Christ (the channel through whom they reach us)" — Father and Son named together as the one source of grace, a witness to the divine dignity of Christ.

The grammatical-historical note the order: grace first, then peace — for peace with God flows from his free favour; there is no true peace apart from grace. The verse displays the apostolic blessing of grace and peace.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's customary greeting — "grace unto you, and peace," grace the source and peace its fruit (Barnes, Robertson); "from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ," Father and Son the joint source, a witness to Christ's dignity (Barnes, Gill); grace first then peace, no peace apart from grace (grammatical-historical). The verse displays grace and peace. The verse marks the blessing: grace and peace. The verse displays the source: Father and Son. The verse displays the order: grace then peace. The verse displays the dignity: Christ with the Father. The blessing displays grace and peace from Father and Son. The verse displays the grace: free favour. The verse displays the peace: its fruit. The verse displays the source: God and Christ. The apostolic blessing of grace and peace displays the opening prayer of the letter: Paul invokes upon the Thessalonians grace — God's free favour — and peace, its fruit, both flowing from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ together. The verse displays the greeting, opening into thanksgiving for their growth (v.3).
3:
"We are bound to thank God always for you, brethren, as it is meet, because that your faith groweth exceedingly, and the charity of every one of you all toward each other aboundeth;" The witnesses note Paul's bound thanksgiving for their growing faith and love. On "we are bound to thank God always for you... as it is meet," the grammatical voices note the obligation. Barnes and Robertson note the strong expression: Paul says he is "bound (under obligation, owe it as a debt) to thank God always for you... as it is meet (fitting, right, due)" — his thanksgiving is not a mere impulse but a debt owed, fitting and proper given their growth.

On "your faith groweth exceedingly," the witnesses note the increase of faith. Barnes and Calvin note the cause: "your faith groweth exceedingly (hyperauxanei — grows beyond measure, abundantly)" — their faith was not static but flourishing, increasing greatly under trial.

On "the charity of every one of you all toward each other aboundeth," the puritan Henry notes the abounding love: "the charity (love) of every one of you all toward each other aboundeth (overflows)" — their mutual love grew along with their faith; faith and love increasing together, and in every member. The verse displays Paul's bound thanksgiving for their growing faith and love.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul is "bound (owes it as a debt) to thank God always for you, as it is meet (fitting)," his thanksgiving a due debt (Barnes, Robertson); "your faith groweth exceedingly (beyond measure)," flourishing under trial (Barnes, Calvin); "the charity (love) of every one toward each other aboundeth (overflows)," mutual love growing with faith, in every member (Henry). The verse displays growing faith and love. The verse marks the obligation: bound to thank. The verse displays the faith: growing exceedingly. The verse displays the love: abounding. The verse displays the scope: every one. The thanksgiving displays Paul thankful for their flourishing graces. The verse displays the thanks: a debt. The verse displays the faith: increasing. The verse displays the love: overflowing. Paul's bound thanksgiving for their growing faith and love displays the cause of his gratitude: he owes God thanks, as is fitting, because the Thessalonians' faith was flourishing beyond measure and the love of every one of them toward each other was overflowing — faith and love increasing together under persecution. The verse displays their flourishing graces, the ground of Paul's boast (v.4).
4:
"So that we ourselves glory in you in the churches of God for your patience and faith in all your persecutions and tribulations that ye endure:" The witnesses note Paul glorying in their patience and faith under persecution. On "we ourselves glory in you in the churches of God," the grammatical voices note the boast. Barnes and Robertson note that their growth was so marked that Paul "ourselves glory (boast, take pride) in you in the churches of God" — he held the Thessalonians up before other churches as an example to be admired and imitated.

On "for your patience and faith," the witnesses note the graces praised. Barnes and Calvin note the ground of his boast: "your patience (steadfast endurance) and faith" — their constancy and steadfast believing under trial; faith that held firm and endurance that did not give way.

On "in all your persecutions and tribulations that ye endure," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the trials: their patience and faith were shown "in all your persecutions and tribulations that ye endure" — amid present, ongoing sufferings; their graces shone the brighter against the dark background of affliction. The verse displays Paul glorying in their patience and faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "ourselves glory (boast) in you in the churches of God," holding them up as an example to others (Barnes, Robertson); "your patience (steadfast endurance) and faith," their constancy and steadfast believing under trial (Barnes, Calvin); "in all your persecutions and tribulations that ye endure," amid ongoing sufferings, their graces shining against affliction (Wesley). The verse displays his boast. The verse marks the glory: in you. The verse displays the audience: the churches of God. The verse displays the graces: patience and faith. The verse displays the trials: persecutions endured. The boast displays Paul praising their steadfastness to other churches. The verse displays the glorying: in them. The verse displays the patience: steadfast. The verse displays the persecutions: endured. Paul glorying in their patience and faith under persecution displays the height of his approval: their growth was so marked that he boasted of them before the other churches of God — pointing to the steadfast endurance and faith they showed amid all their ongoing persecutions and tribulations. The verse displays their endurance, a token of God's righteous judgment (v.5).
5:
"Which is a manifest token of the righteous judgment of God, that ye may be counted worthy of the kingdom of God, for which ye also suffer:" The witnesses note their endurance as a token of God's righteous judgment. On "a manifest token of the righteous judgment of God," the grammatical voices note the sign. Barnes and Robertson note that their patient endurance is "a manifest token (evidence, proof) of the righteous judgment of God" — their suffering grace, and the persecution they bore, point forward to a coming just judgment in which God will set all things right.

On "that ye may be counted worthy of the kingdom of God," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note the design: "that ye may be counted worthy (accounted, reckoned worthy) of the kingdom of God" — their endurance prepares and fits them for the kingdom; God's purpose in their suffering is to ripen them for glory, not that they earn it but that they be reckoned fit.

On "for which ye also suffer," the puritan Henry notes the cause: it is "for which (the kingdom) ye also suffer" — their very sufferings are for the kingdom's sake; they endure now in hope of the reward, and the suffering is the path to the crown. The verse displays their endurance as a token of God's righteous judgment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: their endurance is "a manifest token (proof) of the righteous judgment of God," pointing to a coming just judgment that sets all right (Barnes, Robertson); "that ye may be counted worthy (reckoned fit) of the kingdom of God," their suffering ripening them for glory (Barnes, Calvin); "for which ye also suffer," their sufferings for the kingdom's sake, the path to the crown (Henry). The verse displays the token of judgment. The verse marks the sign: token of righteous judgment. The verse displays the purpose: counted worthy. The verse displays the kingdom: their goal. The verse displays the suffering: for it. The token displays their endurance pointing to God's just judgment. The verse displays the judgment: righteous. The verse displays the worthiness: reckoned. The verse displays the suffering: for the kingdom. Their endurance as a token of God's righteous judgment displays the meaning of their suffering: their patient faith under persecution is a sure sign of the coming righteous judgment of God — in which they will be counted worthy of the kingdom for which they now suffer, and their persecutors will be repaid. The verse displays the righteous judgment, repaying the persecutors (v.6).
6:
"Seeing it is a righteous thing with God to recompense tribulation to them that trouble you;" The brief verse notes the justice of God in repaying tribulation to the persecutors. The grammatical voices note the principle of recompense. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of the coming judgment: "it is a righteous thing with God (just in his sight) to recompense (repay, requite) tribulation to them that trouble you" — divine justice requires that the persecutors be repaid with the very affliction they inflicted.

On "recompense tribulation," the witnesses note the retribution. Barnes and Calvin note the lex talionis of divine justice: God will "recompense tribulation" — repay affliction with affliction; those who troubled the saints will themselves be troubled; the punishment fits the crime.

On "them that trouble you," the Reformed Calvin notes the comfort: the assurance that God will repay the persecutors is meant to comfort the afflicted — they need not avenge themselves, for God's justice will surely requite their oppressors in due time. The verse displays the justice of God in repaying the persecutors.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground of judgment — "it is a righteous thing with God (just) to recompense (repay) tribulation to them that trouble you," divine justice requiring the persecutors be repaid (Barnes, Robertson); the retribution fitting the crime, affliction repaid with affliction (Barnes, Calvin); the assurance comforting the afflicted, who need not avenge themselves (Calvin). The verse displays the just recompense. The verse marks the justice: a righteous thing. The verse displays the recompense: tribulation. The verse displays the objects: the persecutors. The verse displays the comfort: God will repay. The justice displays God repaying the persecutors in kind. The verse displays the righteousness: of God. The verse displays the recompense: just. The verse displays the troublers: repaid. The justice of God in repaying tribulation to the persecutors displays the principle of the coming judgment: it is righteous in God's sight to repay with affliction those who afflict his people — a retribution that fits the crime, and a comfort to the suffering saints who may leave vengeance to him. The verse displays the just recompense, answered by rest for the troubled (v.7).
7:
"And to you who are troubled rest with us, when the Lord Jesus shall be revealed from heaven with his mighty angels," The witnesses note the promise of rest to the troubled at Christ's revelation. On "to you who are troubled rest with us," the grammatical voices note the relief. Barnes and Robertson note the other side of the recompense: "to you who are troubled (afflicted, persecuted) rest (anesis — relief, relaxation, ease) with us" — God will repay the afflicted saints, not with more affliction, but with rest; relief from their sufferings, shared with Paul and all the faithful.

On "when the Lord Jesus shall be revealed from heaven," the witnesses note the time. Barnes and Calvin note that this rest comes "when the Lord Jesus shall be revealed (apokalypsis — unveiled, disclosed) from heaven" — at Christ's glorious second coming, when he is unveiled in majesty; the recompense, both of rest and of judgment, awaits that day.

On "with his mighty angels," the grammatical-historical note the attendants: Christ comes "with his mighty angels (the angels of his power)" — attended by the angelic host as the ministers of his glory and judgment. The verse displays the promise of rest to the troubled at Christ's revelation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the other side of recompense — "to you who are troubled (afflicted) rest (relief, ease) with us," God repaying the afflicted with rest shared with the faithful (Barnes, Robertson); "when the Lord Jesus shall be revealed (unveiled) from heaven," at Christ's glorious coming (Barnes, Calvin); "with his mighty angels," the angelic host attending his glory and judgment (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promised rest. The verse marks the rest: to the troubled. The verse displays the sharing: with us. The verse displays the time: Christ revealed. The verse displays the attendants: mighty angels. The promise displays the afflicted given rest at Christ's coming. The verse displays the rest: promised. The verse displays the revelation: from heaven. The verse displays the angels: mighty. The promise of rest to the troubled at Christ's revelation displays the believer's hope under persecution: God will repay the afflicted saints with rest — relief from all their sufferings — when the Lord Jesus is unveiled from heaven with his mighty angels. The verse displays the coming of Christ, in flaming fire upon the disobedient (v.8).
8:
"In flaming fire taking vengeance on them that know not God, and that obey not the gospel of our Lord Jesus Christ:" The witnesses note the vengeance on those who know not God and obey not the gospel. On "in flaming fire taking vengeance," the grammatical voices note the judgment. Barnes and Robertson note the awful scene of Christ's coming: "in flaming fire (the symbol of his glory and his consuming judgment) taking vengeance (rendering recompense, executing just retribution)" — Christ comes to judge, repaying the wicked with righteous punishment.

On "them that know not God," the witnesses note the first class. Barnes and Calvin note the first object: "them that know not God" — those who, having the means to know him, remained in willful ignorance of God; chiefly the heathen who rejected the light of nature and conscience.

On "that obey not the gospel of our Lord Jesus Christ," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the second class: "that obey not (refuse to submit to) the gospel of our Lord Jesus Christ" — those who heard the gospel and would not obey it; the gospel demands obedience, and its rejecters fall under judgment. The verse displays the vengeance on those who know not God and obey not the gospel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the awful judgment — "in flaming fire (his consuming glory) taking vengeance (just retribution)," Christ coming to repay the wicked (Barnes, Robertson); "them that know not God," those in willful ignorance, chiefly the heathen rejecting the light (Barnes, Calvin); "that obey not the gospel," those who heard and refused to submit, the gospel demanding obedience (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vengeance. The verse marks the fire: flaming. The verse displays the vengeance: just retribution. The verse displays the first class: know not God. The verse displays the second: obey not the gospel. The judgment displays Christ repaying the ignorant and disobedient. The verse displays the fire: of judgment. The verse displays the ignorance: willful. The verse displays the disobedience: to the gospel. The vengeance on those who know not God and obey not the gospel displays the dread side of Christ's coming: he comes in flaming fire to render just retribution upon those who remained in willful ignorance of God and those who heard the gospel yet would not obey it. The verse displays the judgment of the wicked, whose sentence is everlasting destruction (v.9).
9:
"Who shall be punished with everlasting destruction from the presence of the Lord, and from the glory of his power;" The witnesses note the sentence of everlasting destruction from the Lord's presence. On "punished with everlasting destruction," the grammatical voices note the sentence. Barnes and Robertson note the doom of the wicked: "who shall be punished (pay the penalty, suffer justice) with everlasting destruction (olethron aionion — eternal ruin)" — a punishment that is everlasting in duration, the ruin of the impenitent.

On "everlasting destruction," the witnesses note the nature. Barnes and Calvin note the awful word: "everlasting destruction" — not annihilation but eternal ruin, the loss of all good and the endurance of the penalty without end; the most solemn statement of the doom of the lost.

On "from the presence of the Lord, and from the glory of his power," the eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical note the essence of the punishment: it is "from (away from, banished from) the presence of the Lord, and from the glory of his power" — the heart of the doom is exclusion from God's presence; to be banished from the face and glory of the Lord is itself the deepest ruin. The verse displays the sentence of everlasting destruction.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the doom — "punished (pay the penalty) with everlasting destruction (eternal ruin)," a punishment everlasting in duration (Barnes, Robertson); "everlasting destruction" not annihilation but eternal ruin, the loss of all good without end (Barnes, Calvin); "from the presence of the Lord, and from the glory of his power," the heart of the doom being banishment from God's presence (Chrysostom, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sentence. The verse marks the punishment: everlasting destruction. The verse displays the nature: eternal ruin. The verse displays the essence: from the Lord's presence. The verse displays the loss: God's glory. The sentence displays the wicked banished into eternal ruin. The verse displays the destruction: everlasting. The verse displays the presence: lost. The verse displays the glory: forfeited. The sentence of everlasting destruction from the Lord's presence displays the doom of the impenitent: they shall suffer eternal ruin — not annihilation but unending loss — the very heart of which is banishment from the presence of the Lord and from the glory of his power. The verse displays the doom of the lost, over against the glory of Christ in his saints (v.10).
10:
"When he shall come to be glorified in his saints, and to be admired in all them that believe (because our testimony among you was believed) in that day." The witnesses note Christ glorified and admired in his saints at his coming. On "when he shall come to be glorified in his saints," the grammatical voices note the purpose of his coming. Barnes and Robertson note the bright side of that day: Christ comes "to be glorified in his saints" — his glory will shine forth in the redeemed; the saints will reflect and display his glory, being the trophies of his grace.

On "to be admired in all them that believe," the witnesses note the wonder. Barnes and Calvin note the parallel: "to be admired (marvelled at, wondered at) in all them that believe" — Christ will be the object of wondering admiration as his work in the believers is fully revealed; the glory of his grace in them will excite the wonder of all.

On "because our testimony among you was believed... in that day," the grammatical-historical note the assurance: the Thessalonians are included among these, "because our testimony among you was believed" — they had received Paul's witness, and so would share in the glory of that day. The verse displays Christ glorified and admired in his saints.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the bright side of that day — Christ "to be glorified in his saints," his glory shining forth in the redeemed, the trophies of his grace (Barnes, Robertson); "to be admired (marvelled at) in all them that believe," the object of wondering admiration as his work in them is revealed (Barnes, Calvin); the Thessalonians included "because our testimony was believed," sharing the glory of that day (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Christ glorified in his saints. The verse marks the coming: that day. The verse displays the glory: in his saints. The verse displays the wonder: admired in believers. The verse displays the assurance: testimony believed. The coming displays Christ's glory shining in the redeemed. The verse displays the glorifying: in saints. The verse displays the admiring: in believers. The verse displays the testimony: received. Christ glorified and admired in his saints at his coming displays the glory that answers the judgment: on that day Christ will be glorified in the redeemed and marvelled at in all who believe — among them the Thessalonians, who had believed Paul's testimony — the saints themselves the shining trophies of his grace. The verse displays the glory of that day, for which Paul prays they be fitted (vv.11-12).
11:
"Wherefore also we pray always for you, that our God would count you worthy of this calling, and fulfil all the good pleasure of his goodness, and the work of faith with power:" The witnesses note Paul's prayer that God count them worthy and fulfil his good pleasure in them. On "we pray always for you, that our God would count you worthy of this calling," the grammatical voices note the petition. Barnes and Robertson note that the prospect of that day moves Paul to pray: "that our God would count you worthy (reckon you fit, account you worthy) of this calling" — that they may be found fit for the high calling to which God summoned them, the calling to his kingdom and glory.

On "fulfil all the good pleasure of his goodness," the witnesses note the desire. Barnes and Calvin note the petition: that God would "fulfil (fully accomplish, bring to completion) all the good pleasure of his goodness" — every gracious purpose his goodness forms toward them, every good desire and resolve God works in them, brought to full effect.

On "and the work of faith with power," the puritan Henry notes the further request: that God would fulfil "the work of faith with power" — the work that faith produces, completed by divine power; faith made effective and fruitful by God's own energy. The verse displays Paul's prayer that God count them worthy and fulfil his good pleasure.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the prospect of that day moves Paul to pray "that our God would count you worthy (reckon fit) of this calling," found fit for the calling to his kingdom (Barnes, Robertson); "fulfil all the good pleasure of his goodness," every gracious purpose his goodness forms brought to full effect (Barnes, Calvin); "the work of faith with power," faith made fruitful by divine power (Henry). The verse displays his prayer. The verse marks the petition: count you worthy. The verse displays the calling: high. The verse displays the desire: fulfil his goodness. The verse displays the work: of faith with power. The prayer displays Paul asking God to fit them for glory. The verse displays the worthiness: reckoned. The verse displays the goodness: fulfilled. The verse displays the faith: empowered. Paul's prayer that God count them worthy and fulfil his good pleasure in them displays the goal of his intercession: that God would reckon the Thessalonians fit for their high calling, accomplish in them every gracious purpose of his goodness, and complete the work of their faith by his own power. The verse displays the prayer for their fitness, that Christ may be glorified in them (v.12).
12:
"That the name of our Lord Jesus Christ may be glorified in you, and ye in him, according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ." The witnesses note the mutual glorifying of Christ in them and them in Christ, by grace. On "that the name of our Lord Jesus Christ may be glorified in you," the grammatical voices note the chief end. Barnes and Robertson note the ultimate aim of Paul's prayer: "that the name of our Lord Jesus Christ may be glorified in you" — that Christ's name (his whole revealed person and character) may be honoured and displayed in the believers' lives; their fitness for glory exists for Christ's glory.

On "and ye in him," the witnesses note the reciprocity. Barnes and Calvin note the mutual glory: "and ye in him" — as Christ is glorified in them, so they are glorified in him; a mutual, reciprocal glory, the believers sharing in Christ's honour as he is honoured in them.

On "according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ," the eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the source: all this is "according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ" — the whole work, from first to last, flows from grace, not merit; the mutual glory is grace's gift. The verse displays the mutual glorifying of Christ in them and them in Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the chief end — "that the name of our Lord Jesus Christ may be glorified in you," Christ's name honoured in their lives (Barnes, Robertson); "and ye in him," the mutual reciprocal glory, they sharing his honour (Barnes, Calvin); "according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ," the whole flowing from grace not merit (Cyril, Calvin). The verse displays mutual glory. The verse marks the end: Christ glorified in them. The verse displays the reciprocity: ye in him. The verse displays the source: grace. The verse displays the joint name: God and the Lord Jesus. The glory displays Christ and the saints glorified in each other by grace. The verse displays the name: glorified. The verse displays the mutual: in him. The verse displays the grace: its source. The mutual glorifying of Christ in them and them in Christ, by grace, displays the crown of Paul's prayer: that the name of the Lord Jesus may be glorified in the believers, and they in him — a mutual glory flowing wholly from the grace of God and the Lord Jesus Christ. The verse displays the glory of grace, closing the chapter and opening the teaching on the day of the Lord (ch.2).
